dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for an overdose call at 4109 on 2121 Main Street.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what brought you to call 911 today?
He was found unresponsive earlier, and he has a known history of opioid abuse.
Patient is unresponsive, no response to pain or verbal stimuli.
Eyes do not open, and pupils are pinpoint and unreactive.
No motor response when various stimulation is applied, GCS of 3.
The airway is patent, but respirations are shallow and inadequate.
There's equal bilateral chest rise and fall, rate about 4 per minute with snoring sounds.
Strong, regular bilateral carotid and radial pulses are present.
Patient has a documented opioid abuse history. Bystander confirmed possible heroin use.
Patient was found unresponsive approximately 20 minutes ago. CPR was performed by RFD responders.
Bystander reported no specific complaints from the patient pre-unresponsiveness or post-Narcan administration.
Pulse is 90, blood pressure 151 over 89, respiratory rate 12.
SpO₂ is at 95%, capillary refill is less than 2 seconds.
Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color with no visible rashes or bites noted.
Initiating assisted ventilation via BVM with high-flow oxygen.
Inserting a nasopharyngeal airway for improved airway management.
RFD performed CPR for unresponsive patient with inadequate respirations, rescues with breaths discontinued when EMS arrived and found pulses present.
Blood glucose is 110 mg/dL, and temperature is 98.0°F.
Positive PMS noted; pupils are pinpoint and equal.
Administering 1mg Narcan intranasally per standing order now.
Respiratory rate remains 12; SpO₂ is now 100%, pulse at 86.
Blood pressure is now 119 over 83 post-Narcan administration.
Applying a 4-lead ECG for continuous cardiac monitoring.
Completed a 12-lead EKG showing sinus rhythm with STT abnormalities and high QRS voltages.
Blood pressure is elevated to 154 over 117 with SpO₂ at 94%.
Respiratory rate is increased to 18, and patient shows signs of improved arousability.
Patient is arousable, around 13 GCS score due to fatigue during en route, but not fully oriented x4.
No patient verbal response to pain questions.
Pupils remain pinpoint after first dose, not reactive to light.
Rechecking 12-lead, showing occasional ventricular premature complexes possibly.
Skin remains warm and dry with no signs of rashes or bites.
Patient denies chest pain, shortness of breath, but was unresponsive prior to Narcan.
Past medical history includes opioid abuse only. No known allergies.
Exiting protocol: Medical - Overdose/POISONING - Opioid (Protocol 7-1).
12-lead ECG consistent with minimal left ventricular hypertrophy.
Best destination is VCU ED for follow-up.
Patient is wrapped in a blanket and moved to the stretcher to initiate transport.
Patient is secured in the ambulance with straps for safe transport.
Notifying VCU ED of overdose with heroin use, Narcan administered, and EKG abnormalities.
Patient is carried into the ambulance and secured in transit.
Patient denied IV and refused ACMS signature.
ED RN accepted care and signed on-staff behalf.
Patient voluntarily slid from EMS stretcher to hospital cot upon arrival.
Final handoff report delivered to ED RN, including vitals, Narcan administration, and EKG findings.
Ambulance is at the ED doors. Patient is safely unloaded.
Confirmed patient is off the stretcher and now in hospital care.
Final vitals: pulse 91, BP 154/117, SpO₂ 94%.
Handoff to nursing staff is complete. Notified ED of planned follow-up.
Unit 3 clears the call after transporting the overdose patient and effecting Narcan administration.
